# Human Review Flags

**Auto-generated from the executed pipeline run** (`outputs/06_human_review_flags.json` + the red-team issue list in `outputs/05_quantification.json`). This file is the answer to "where should the AI output not be trusted without human review" — every entry below was raised *by the pipeline itself*, not added after the fact.

Total flags: **23** auto-collected + **9** red-team issues.

## Post-research status (updated 2026-07-06)

A second agentic pass (6 web-research verifiers + an assessor review, findings in [outputs/09_research_validation.json](outputs/09_research_validation.json), adjudication in [SOURCES.md](SOURCES.md)) worked the flags below. Status of the major themes:

| Theme | Status |
|-------|--------|
| Quantitative parameters are unverified model memory | **Worked.** Every baseline/effectiveness verified against DBIR 2026, GitGuardian 2026, Sophos 2025, Proofpoint, NetDiligence 2025, Picus 2025; five parameters recalibrated → v1.1 model (56.7% → 20.5%). Remaining: validate against the company's own telemetry; human click-through of the ~30 cited URLs |
| Prices are model-memory estimates | **Worked.** 13 SKUs checked against live vendor pages 2026-07-06; **5 corrected** (Entra P2 $10, EPM $3.00, "Defender Suite" rename, Defender-for-App-Service bundle → ~$44/mo, FIDO2 keys $58/$29), plus a confirmation that matters (GitHub Secret Protection needs no Enterprise). Remaining: negotiated quotes |
| Joint calibration: baselines imply ~65%/yr, high vs SMB base rates | **Worked.** v1.1 lands at 56.7%, inside the red team's suggested ceiling — external research and the internal skeptic converged |
| R3+R5 contradictory control states; cross-risk double counting | **Partially worked.** v1.1 recalibration reduces the double-counted mass (R3 mode nearly halved); the structural fix (a portfolio-reconciliation stage) is specified in the README's pipeline changelog for the next run |
| Challenger dissent on the #1 ranking (R3 vs R1) | **Worked analytically.** The two-sided framework-sensitivity sweep shows R3 stays #1 across impact weights 0.30–0.70 (including the secondary-dominant end) and under mean-vs-median aggregation ([outputs/08](outputs/08_advanced_analytics.json)); the R1-vs-R2 pair is flagged as a statistical tie. The likelihood-conditioning critique itself still deserves a human read |
| Notification-duty materiality assumption | **Open.** One conversation with counsel; no research substitute |
| SOC 2 criteria mappings | **Worked.** Audited against AICPA TSC text; two criteria dropped, two added — see [ROADMAP.md](ROADMAP.md) |
| Effectiveness assumes one 3-person team implements all five packages | **Open** (structural). Mitigated only by the roadmap's phased waves; track via the KRI review cadence |

## Standing pipeline flag

- **[ALL]** STANDING FLAG: every numeric parameter (baselines, effectiveness, costs) is an LLM estimate from model memory. Validate against current DBIR/industry reports, actual Azure/M365 pricing, and the company's own telemetry before using in budget or board material.

## Adversarial challenge (ranking)

- **[R3]** A challenger refuted the #1 ranking (alternative: R1, confidence medium): R3's #1 ranking rests on a likelihood score (L=4.0) that the audit trail itself contradicts in two ways: a TEF/VULN conditioning mismatch that double-counts chain success, and a TEF contributor that cannot apply to private repos.

(1) Conditioning mismatch between TEF and VULN. The two lenses that s

## Red team — correlation / independence concerns

- **[ALL]** Independence-assumption concern: Shared endpoint-compromise driver: the same infostealer infection on the unmanaged, local-admin, no-EDR laptop fleet is the dominant input to R3 (PAT/secret harvest), the primary vector for R4 (ransomware), and a residual path in R1 (session-cookie theft). Treating these as independent double counts single events, biasing the aggregate P(at least one material incident) UPWARD — probably the largest single distortion in the 1-prod(1-p_i) result.
- **[ALL]** Independence-assumption concern: Shared identity weakness: phishable push MFA with no Conditional Access underlies R1's ATO baseline, R3's phished-Entra-portal residual, and R5's credential-reuse-against-dormant-accounts scenario. These baselines co-move — if R1's phishing-resistant MFA package lands, R3 and R5 baselines both drop (R5's own assumptions admit this) — so the risks are functionally coupled through a common control, not independent.
- **[ALL]** Independence-assumption concern: Common-cause detection failure: no SIEM, no EDR monitoring, and business-hours-only triage by 3 people is the single mechanism that converts incidents into MATERIAL incidents in all five bases. This positive correlation means the independence formula overstates P(any incident) but materially UNDERSTATES the joint tail — the probability of multiple simultaneous or cascading material incidents in the same bad year, which is what actually bankrupts a $20M ARR company.
- **[ALL]** Independence-assumption concern: Correlated mitigation under-delivery: all five effectiveness estimates are conditioned on full implementation by the same 3-person team also running SOC 2. Execution slippage is a common-mode failure — if one package stalls, all likely stall — so residual risks should be modeled with a shared execution-quality factor rather than five independent point-estimate reductions; independence here biases the modeled residual portfolio risk DOWNWARD.
- **[ALL]** Independence-assumption concern: Deterministic cross-package dependencies stated by the estimators themselves: R2's effectiveness is conditioned on contractor-DB cleanup landing, R3's Postgres lockdown is gated on R5's work, and R3 vs R5 assume opposite states of Postgres Entra-auth. These are functional dependencies between residuals, so P(breach) = 1 - prod(1 - p_i) over residuals is structurally wrong for this set regardless of parameter values.
- **[ALL]** Independence-assumption concern: Net direction: for the headline annual P(>=1 material incident), the independence assumption biases the number UP (shared events counted in multiple risks); for tail/joint outcomes and for realized effectiveness, it biases risk DOWN (correlated detection failure and correlated implementation slippage). A decision-maker using the headline number sees inflated frequency but dangerously understated worst-case correlation.

## Quantification estimators (self-declared)

- **[R3]** All numeric anchors come from LLM memory, not verified sources. A human must: (1) check current-edition Verizon DBIR credential-abuse/System-Intrusion prevalence, IBM Cost of a Data Breach initial-vector shares, and GitGuardian secrets-sprawl figures against the values implied in the basis text; (2) validate the dominant chain input — annual infostealer compromise probability for a ~60-dev, local-admin, no-EDR fleet — against current threat-intel (this single number moves the baseline mode by 2-3x); (3) replace the assumed exposure with ground truth by actually running the gitleaks/trufflehog sweep — if zero live secrets are found the baseline drops sharply, if many are live it rises; (4) confirm the materiality mapping (that DB/backup access triggers notification duty under the applicable jurisdictions for the 500k data subjects); (5) confirm which feasibility descopes actually happen (GitHub plan/SSO, Postgres Entra-auth deferral, quick-win #4 gating) since effectiveness was estimated against the adjusted, not original, package; and (6) ensure the portfolio model does not double-count the shared infostealer precondition across R3, R5, and the endpoint/identity risks.
- **[R1]** All quantitative figures are LLM calibration judgments from model memory, not measured rates. A human must: (1) verify the cited industry patterns against the CURRENT editions of Verizon DBIR, Microsoft Digital Defense Report, IBM Cost of a Data Breach, and FBI IC3 — especially phishing/credential-abuse prevalence and AiTM/PhaaS growth claims, which may have shifted since the model's Jan 2026 cutoff; (2) sanity-check the baseline mode (30%/yr material incident) against any available broker/cyber-insurance actuarial data for 100-500 employee SaaS firms with phishable MFA, since no public source reports this exact conditional probability and it is the single most influential judgment call; (3) validate the >90% high-end effectiveness claim by confirming the org actually enforces the phishing-resistant authentication-strength CA policy tenant-wide (not report-only), that TAP issuance verification is operating, and that local-password login is disabled on all 15 SSO apps — the estimate collapses toward the 60% low if any of these silently fail; (4) confirm the scope boundary with R4: endpoint token theft was excluded from R1's effectiveness on the assumption the downstream model treats it under R4, so double-counting or gaps between R1/R4 must be checked at the aggregation layer; (5) review the assumption that partial-year enforcement (full CA by month 4) is compatible with the prompt's 'operated for a full year' framing — if the model should assume day-1 full operation, mode effectiveness could reasonably be 5 points higher.
- **[R2]** LLM-derived quantities are the least trustworthy artifact in this pipeline. A human must verify: (1) the industry anchors against CURRENT editions — Verizon DBIR web-app-attack and vulnerability-exploitation prevalence, Hiscox/Ponemon SMB annual incident rates, IBM Cost-of-a-Breach per-record and notification-cost figures — since all numbers here are from model memory and may be stale or misremembered; (2) the baseline mode of 20%/yr against any company-specific evidence that would move it sharply (results of the first ZAP baseline scan, initial Dependabot critical count, any prior pen-test or bug-bounty findings, WAF-detection-mode hit logs once available — these are direct observations that should override this prior); (3) the assumption that essentially any successful app-layer exfiltration is material given US state / GDPR-equivalent notification duties for the actual end-user population; (4) the effectiveness mode of 65%, especially the split between commodity (WAF-addressable) and targeted IDOR/logic (largely non-addressable) threat share, which is the single biggest lever in the estimate and is not empirically grounded; (5) the RLS-ships-this-year and backlog-burn-down assumptions with the Head of Engineering, since the feasibility review itself flags both as at-risk; and (6) the R3 dependency — if contractor direct-DB accounts are not cleaned up, cap effectiveness credit for the RLS control. Recommend re-estimating after the 4-week WAF detection-mode data and the initial CodeQL/Dependabot backlog counts exist, as those two artifacts will shrink the uncertainty bands more than any further desk analysis.
- **[R4]** These are LLM-memory estimates, not measured rates, and must be human-verified before the downstream model consumes them. Specifically verify: (1) the industry base rates against the CURRENT editions of Verizon DBIR, Sophos State of Ransomware, NetDiligence Cyber Claims Study, and IBM Cost of a Data Breach — the recalled figures (ransomware in ~1/3-44% of breaches; ~50-60% SMB attack exposure; mid-single-digit to low-teens material-incident conversion) may be stale or misremembered; (2) the 2-3x below-average-maturity uplift, which is analyst judgment with no citable source — a human should sanity-check it against cyber-insurance underwriting benchmarks for orgs lacking EDR/MFA-strength controls; (3) the R1/R4 scope boundary, since double-counting the phishing-origin vs malware-origin ransomware paths across the 5 risks would inflate the aggregate; (4) the effectiveness discount structure — especially the assumptions that enrollment reaches ~95%, AIR stays on Full, and the 4-5 month calendar holds despite SOC 2 consuming ~20% of the same two sysadmins; if the month-4 hard deadline slips and descoping occurs, use the 45% low, not the mode; (5) the materiality threshold mapping — confirm that a same-day Files Restore rollback with no exfiltration would genuinely stay under $25k IR cost for this org, because that assumption does significant work in the corrective control's contribution to effectiveness.
- **[R5]** All figures are LLM judgment calibrated against statistics recalled from memory, not retrieved sources. A human must: (1) verify the cited industry patterns against the CURRENT editions of Verizon DBIR (valid-credential and Privilege Misuse pattern prevalence), Ponemon/DTEX insider-threat reports, and any ex-employee-access survey relied on — the recalled percentages may be wrong or outdated; (2) replace the assumed turnover rate with Meridian's actual departure count and the actual number of contractors holding direct Postgres logins, which dominate the baseline; (3) confirm the materiality mapping — whether an export of customer tables genuinely triggers notification duties in the relevant jurisdictions (this single assumption moves the baseline several points); (4) validate the effectiveness ceiling against the final implemented scope (was the Postgres Entra-auth cutover completed for ALL human accounts? is the weekly HR reconciliation actually running?), since the estimate assumes the adjusted, not original, package; (5) check for interaction with the MFA/Conditional Access package to avoid double-counting risk reduction across the five estimates, and confirm R3 and R5 assume the SAME Postgres Entra-auth end state; (6) sanity-check that low/mode/high orderings and the annual-probability interpretation match the downstream model's expected parameterization; (7) review the two judgment calls introduced in this revision — the documented departure from panel likelihood parity with R4, and the ~70-75%/25-30% post- vs pre-departure baseline split that caps blended effectiveness at ~57% — both materially move the numbers and rest on unverified assumptions about departure counts and insider-theft timing.

## IT feasibility review (non-approve verdicts)

- **[R3]** IT feasibility verdict "approve_with_changes": Resolve the GitHub plan question before funding: confirm current plan and actual active-committer count. If on Team, either budget the Enterprise Cloud upgrade explicitly (~$15k/yr) or replace SAML SSO enforcement with enforced 2FA + classic-PAT disablement + fine-grained PAT policy (available on Te
- **[R1]** IT feasibility verdict "approve_with_changes": Split Intune enrollment (60% -> target ~95% of corporate devices) into its own workstream shared with R4, with an explicit BYOD policy decision (enroll, stipend, or web-only forever). Re-state this package's compliant-device control honestly: 'app-enforced restrictions (block download) is the end-st
- **[R2]** IT feasibility verdict "approve_with_changes": Gate the RLS retrofit on the 2-day schema/pooling spike BEFORE committing budget or the M t-shirt: spike must confirm tenant_id coverage on target tables and that per-request tenant context survives the app's connection-pooling mode. Re-baseline person-weeks after the spike; treat 'top 10-20 tables'
- **[R4]** IT feasibility verdict "approve_with_changes": Re-baseline effort to L / ~12 person-weeks with an explicit 4-5 month calendar plan, and add the permanent ops line item (~3 hrs/week EDR triage rota + monthly compliance report) to the team's standing workload before approval.; Move EPM from 'optional' to budgeted-mandatory for the 60 engineers (~$
- **[R5]** IT feasibility verdict "approve_with_changes": Split into three phases with a hard gate. Phase 1 (weeks 1-2, before anything else): all five quick wins — HR-roster-to-Entra/GitHub/pg_roles reconciliation, ALTER ROLE VALID UNTIL on human DB accounts, contractor account expirations, connection-log alerting, and the named-systems offboarding checkl

## Mitigation architects (self-declared)

- **[R3]** Verify before funding: (1) GitHub plan compatibility — the Secret Protection add-on ($19/committer/mo, standalone since April 2025) requires GitHub Team or Enterprise Cloud; confirm the actual active-committer count (billed metric) rather than assuming all 60 engineers, and confirm SAML SSO enforcement is available on the current plan (Enterprise Cloud only — this may be the hidden cost driver, ~$21/user/mo, and should be priced explicitly if they are on Team). (2) App feasibility of Entra ID auth to PostgreSQL — driver/token-refresh support in their language stack; if impractical short-term, fall back to Key Vault-stored passwords with 90-day rotation and keep the network lockdown. (3) Defender for Storage is per-transaction-priced on some plans — model against actual Blob transaction volume and cap malware-scanning GB to avoid bill surprises. (4) Confirm Azure Backup long-term retention for PostgreSQL Flexible Server is available in their region; otherwise use pg_dump-to-immutable-blob as the isolation mechanism. (5) Immutability policy duration is irreversible once locked — legal/finance should agree retention length first. (6) Sequence the work so OIDC migration and secret rotation land before the SOC 2 Type I evidence window opens (~6 months out), and check whether their auditor wants the runbook tabletop documented as a test. (7) Sanity-check that engineering can absorb ~9 person-weeks alongside roadmap; the two-week primary control (OIDC + RBAC downscope) should be funded even if the rest slips a quarter.
- **[R1]** Verify E5 Security add-on pricing and eligibility on the current CSP/EA agreement (~$12/user/mo list as of early 2026; requires an eligible base license like M365 E3 — confirm all 200 seats qualify, and compare against buying Entra ID P2 + Defender for Office P2 separately, which is usually more expensive). Confirm passkey feasibility for the remote workforce: device-bound passkeys in Authenticator need reasonably current iOS/Android — decide policy for staff who refuse Authenticator on personal phones (issue ~$25-60 FIDO2 keys instead; budget accordingly). Stage every CA policy in report-only mode for 1-2 weeks and validate against the 15 SSO apps — some older SAML apps mishandle auth-strength or device-compliance claims and could lock users out. Confirm at least two break-glass accounts excluded from CA with keys in a safe, or the IT Manager can lock the whole tenant out. Check the Intune enrollment plan for the unenrolled 40% (corporate vs BYOD split changes whether full device compliance is even achievable). Finally, confirm the SOC 2 auditor accepts Microsoft-native alerting/XDR as the monitoring control for CC7.2 in lieu of a SIEM — most do at this company size, but ask before the Type I window.
- **[R2]** Before funding: (1) Price Front Door Premium against Application Gateway WAF v2 using actual monthly egress/request volume — for a single-region App Service the App Gateway option is often cheaper, but Front Door Premium bundles bot protection and managed-rule auto-updates; also confirm the app has no websocket/large-upload/long-poll patterns that need WAF exclusions before Prevention mode. (2) GitHub Code Security billing is per unique active committer on enabled repos and requires GitHub Team or Enterprise Cloud — count real committers on the product repos (may be well under 60) and confirm the current GitHub plan before accepting the $14-22k figure. (3) Confirm Defender for open-source relational databases supports the exact Flexible Server tier in use and estimate Log Analytics ingestion GB/day (pgAudit on a busy DB can get expensive; start with ddl+role audit classes, not full read logging). (4) The RLS retrofit estimate assumes tenant_id exists on target tables — have the Head of Engineering scope a 2-day spike first; if the schema lacks consistent tenant keys, person-weeks grow materially and the t-shirt becomes L. (5) Book the pen-test vendor now: quality boutiques have 6-10 week lead times and the report must precede the SOC 2 Type I window. (6) Check customer SLAs before enabling statement_timeout on the app role to avoid breaking legitimate long-running reports.
- **[R4]** Confirm Defender for Business eligibility (hard cap at 300 seats — at 200 they qualify, but check headcount growth plans; past 300 the fallback is Defender for Endpoint P2 add-on at ~$5.20/user/mo, roughly doubling license cost) and get current CSP pricing rather than list price. Verify the fleet OS mix — the plan and person-weeks assume mostly Windows; Macs change tooling and effort. Pressure-test the local-admin removal with Engineering leadership before committing a date: 60 engineers who compile and install tooling daily are the failure mode, so decide up front between a documented exception group (free, weaker) and Intune EPM (~$2.6k/yr, cleaner audit story). Check that CA enforcement is sequenced after an exception path for contractors/executives exists, or day one will be an outage. Confirm OneDrive Known Folder Move and versioning storage headroom against the tenant's SharePoint quota. Finally, align the enforcement milestones with the SOC 2 Type I evidence window (~6 months out): auditors need the controls operating, not just designed, so CA enforcement and EDR coverage should land at least 6-8 weeks before fieldwork.
- **[R5]** Verify before funding: (1) Entra ID Governance licensing - confirm current per-user price and whether Microsoft requires licensing all users or only governed ones; if the ~$17k/yr is unpalatable, the Logic App/PowerShell leaver runbook plus P2 scoped to ~80 reviewed users delivers ~90% of the value for ~$8.6k, at the cost of hand-built audit evidence. (2) GitHub plan: confirm whether they are on Team or already Enterprise; the ~$12k/yr SSO/SCIM uplift is the most deferrable line item - manual GitHub removal with the checklist is acceptable short-term. (3) Postgres Entra auth: confirm contractor tooling (psql, DBeaver, ORM consoles) handles AAD token auth and the ~60-90 min token lifetime without workflow revolt, and that the application's own connection path is untouched; pilot with 2-3 users first. (4) HR trigger: there is likely no Workday/SuccessFactors connector, so employeeLeaveDateTime will be set manually by IT on hire/contract start - confirm HR will reliably notify IT of end dates, since the whole automation keys off that field. (5) Ask the SOC 2 auditor early what deprovisioning evidence format they expect (workflow logs vs. tickets) so the Type I sample period starts clean. (6) Sanity-check Log Analytics ingestion volume once Postgres logging is on - chatty connection logs can surprise on cost; cap with daily quota.

## Red team — cross-estimate issues (verbatim)

The red-team agent reviews all five quantification estimates *as a set*. Issues tagged with a single risk id triggered an automatic revision of that estimate (R5 was revised); issues spanning multiple risks (`R3+R5`, `ALL`, …) are **structural** and are left for humans — a known limitation of the current pipeline, documented in the README.

- **[R3+R5 · assumptions]** Contradictory assumed states of the same control: R3's effectiveness is explicitly conditioned on Postgres Entra-auth being DEFERRED (Key Vault password fallback, 'NOT Entra-auth'), while R5's 65% effectiveness mode depends on 'Entra-only Postgres auth' being completed for ALL human accounts within the year. Both cannot be true; whichever is wrong shifts that risk's residual materially (R5's dominant kill-chain break disappears, or R3's residual secret-theft surface shrinks).
  - *Suggested correction:* Fix a single canonical implementation state for Postgres authentication (and its timeline) at the portfolio level, then re-condition both effectiveness estimates on it. If Entra-auth is deferred, lower R5 effectiveness mode to roughly 50-55%; if it lands, raise R3's mode a few points and note the shared dependency in the downstream model.
- **[R3+R4 · baseline]** Double counting of the shared infostealer precondition: R3's baseline chain embeds P(>=1 dev-laptop infostealer infection) of 35-60% as its dominant input, and R4's baseline is built on the same infostealer/loader foothold on the same ~60-laptop, local-admin, no-EDR fleet. A single infection event can be monetized as either a PAT/secrets harvest (R3) or ransomware (R4); the downstream 1-prod(1-p_i) formula then counts the same underlying event twice, inflating the aggregate.
  - *Suggested correction:* Model endpoint infection as one shared upstream node and partition monetization outcomes conditionally (P(secrets-harvest path | infection) vs P(ransomware path | infection) summing to <=1), or explicitly deduct the overlap from one of the two baselines (e.g., reduce R4 mode by the share of infections already consumed by R3's chain, roughly 3-5 points).
- **[R1+R4 · assumptions]** Coverage gap between scope boundaries: R1 excludes endpoint-resident token/PRT/session-cookie theft as 'R4 territory', but R4 is scoped strictly to endpoint-origin RANSOMWARE/extortion. An infostealer stealing an Entra session cookie that leads to non-ransomware cloud data exfiltration (M365/SharePoint PII download) falls into neither baseline, so the aggregate understates this plausible material path — the very path R1 cites as its main residual.
  - *Suggested correction:* Assign the endpoint-token-theft-to-cloud-exfiltration path explicitly to one risk (most naturally broaden R4 to 'endpoint-origin compromise' or add it back to R1's baseline with a few points), and record the assignment as a stated assumption in both estimates so the aggregation layer can verify no gap or overlap.
- **[R5 · baseline]** R4 and R5 received identical panel likelihood scores (3.5), yet R5's baseline mode (8%) is roughly half of R4's (15%) — and R5's own basis lists unusually severe aggravators (standing contractor Postgres logins to the crown-jewel dataset, six unlinked revocation points, zero detection). The divergence from panel parity is not reconciled anywhere; either the panel's likelihood rationale or the estimate is wrong.
  - *Suggested correction:* Either raise R5's mode toward ~12-15% (consistent with the ex-employee-incident survey range of 10-25% the basis itself cites, given the aggravators) or add an explicit assumption documenting why the desk estimate departs from the panel's likelihood parity with R4 (e.g., low departure count dominates), so the discrepancy is a decision, not an accident.
- **[R5 · effectiveness]** The 65% effectiveness mode is internally inconsistent with R5's own stated residuals: pre-departure exfiltration by a soon-to-leave contractor — explicitly part of the named baseline scenario ('contractor exports tables after a dispute') — is admitted to be completely unmitigated, SCIM covers only 4-6 of ~15 apps, GitHub PATs survive to expiry, and the Entra-auth dependency conflicts with R3 (see companion issue). If pre-departure insider exfil is even ~25-30% of the baseline mass, arithmetic caps achievable effectiveness near 55-60%.
  - *Suggested correction:* Decompose the R5 baseline into (a) post-departure dormant-credential use and (b) pre-departure exfiltration, state the assumed split, and recompute: effectiveness ~80% on (a) and ~0-10% on (b) yields a blended mode of roughly 50-58%, not 65%.
- **[R2+R3+R5 · assumptions]** Ownership of the contractor direct-PostgreSQL exposure is assigned inconsistently across three estimates: R2 caps its RLS credit pending 'R3's contractor-account cleanup', R3 gates its Postgres network lockdown behind 'the R5 contractor-access work', and R5 treats contractor Postgres logins as its own core scenario. If each estimator assumed another package fixes it, the exposure may be mitigated in zero packages while three effectiveness estimates take partial credit for its removal.
  - *Suggested correction:* Assign the contractor-DB-access remediation to exactly one package (R5 is the natural owner) with a committed timeline, then strip conditional credit from the other two estimates unless that timeline precedes their measurement window; add a cross-reference assumption to all three.
- **[ALL · effectiveness]** Each of the five effectiveness modes (70/80/65/65/65) is conditioned on ITS OWN multi-month package being 'fully implemented AND operated for the year' by the same 3-person IT team (no security engineer) that is simultaneously delivering the other four packages plus SOC 2 Type I (~20% of two sysadmins per R4). The conditions are individually plausible but jointly infeasible — total implementation load far exceeds team capacity — so taking all five modes at face value systematically overstates portfolio-level risk reduction.
  - *Suggested correction:* Either (a) have the downstream model draw effectiveness from correlated distributions with a shared 'execution capacity' factor (with joint realization biased toward each estimate's low end), or (b) sequence the packages explicitly and use partial-year/low-end effectiveness for whichever packages land in the back half of the year.
- **[ALL · baseline]** Joint calibration failure: the five baseline modes (30/20/20/15/8) under the model's independence formula imply ~65% annual probability of at least one material incident, well above the ~20-45% all-vector SMB material-incident rate that R2's own basis cites as a ceiling ('R2 is only one vector, so its slice must be well below that'). Part of this is the double counting flagged above, but even after dedup the set likely sits above the survey ceiling without anyone having sanity-checked the aggregate.
  - *Suggested correction:* Run the aggregate check explicitly: compute 1-prod(1-mode_i), compare to a below-average-maturity-adjusted all-vector benchmark (perhaps 35-55% for this control posture), and proportionally shade baselines — or document why Meridian's posture justifies exceeding the surveyed ceiling — before the deterministic model consumes these numbers.
- **[ALL · assumptions]** Every one of the five baselines is load-bearing on the same unstated assumption: that unauthorized access to the ~500k end-user PII records 'near-automatically' triggers a notification duty and hence materiality. No estimate states the assumed jurisdictional mix of the data subjects (US-state vs GDPR/UK vs other), the controller-vs-processor question (as a B2B processor, Meridian's duty may be contractual notice to 1,200 customer orgs rather than regulator notification), or whether contractual SLAs alone cross $25k. If materiality-given-access drops from ~90% to ~50%, all five baselines drop nearly in half simultaneously.
  - *Suggested correction:* Add a shared, explicit assumption block (used by all five estimates) specifying the data-subject jurisdiction mix, Meridian's processor obligations, and the assumed P(material | unauthorized PII access); have counsel validate it once rather than five separate human-review items.

## Red team — overall assessment (verbatim)

> The set is unusually well-disciplined in ways red teams normally have to demand — every estimate flags LLM-memory provenance, none has an effectiveness mode above 80%, ranges are appropriately wide, and cross-risk ordering broadly tracks the panel (R1 30% > R3/R2 20% > R4 15%), with the one unreconciled exception that R5 (8%) sits at half of R4 despite identical panel likelihood of 3.5. The material defects are structural, not calibration-cowboy: (1) the five scope boundaries do not tile the event space — the infostealer precondition is counted in both R3 and R4, endpoint-cookie-theft-to-cloud-exfil falls into neither R1 nor R4, and the contractor Postgres exposure is claimed/deferred by three different estimates, with R3 and R5 assuming contradictory states of the same Entra-auth control; (2) the effectiveness estimates are individually conditioned on full implementation but jointly infeasible for a 3-person IT team also delivering SOC 2, so portfolio-level risk reduction is overstated; (3) nobody sanity-checked the aggregate — the modes imply ~65%/yr P(>=1 material incident) under independence, above the all-vector SMB ceiling R2 itself cites; and (4) all five baselines lean on one unstated shared assumption (unauthorized PII access near-automatically triggers notification-duty materiality, despite Meridian being a B2B processor) whose failure would halve every number simultaneously. Recommended before the deterministic model consumes these: define a canonical control-implementation timeline and a shared event taxonomy (dedup rule) at the portfolio level, restructure aggregation around shared upstream nodes (endpoint infection, identity weakness, detection absence, execution capacity) instead of five independent Bernoullis, and get one counsel-validated materiality assumption instead of five ad-hoc ones. The independence formula as specified will overstate the headline frequency while understating correlated worst-case outcomes — both errors matter for the prioritization decision this feeds.
